findings.description: get() calls _purge_expired() on every read, turning an O(1) token lookup into a full O(n) scan-plus-list-allocation of the entire session dict on the hottest code path; purge lazily (evict only the fetched session) or throttle the full sweep behind a timestamp/call-count.

_purge_expired() does a full O(n) iteration over all sessions and allocates a throwaway 'dead' list every invocation, and it is invoked eagerly from both get() and active_count() rather than amortized in the background.
active_count() forces a full O(n) purge before returning len(); if polled by a metrics/monitoring loop this couples every scrape to a whole-store scan.

_new_token() makes 32 individual random.choice() calls through a generator per token; '"".join(random.choices(alphabet, k=TOKEN_LENGTH))' is a single C-level call and several times faster on the create() path.

user_sessions() linearly scans every session to filter by user_id with no secondary user_id->tokens index; a latent quadratic risk if called inside a loop, severity depends on call frequency not visible from this single file.
Tokens are generated from the non-cryptographic random (Mersenne Twister), which is predictable for session identifiers; deferring detail to critic-security, noted here only as out-of-lane.
